item.title: 哈佛史上最噁畢業演說，小粉紅裝腔作勢大外宣！美籍華裔學生完勝蔣雨融...
item.itunes:title: 哈佛史上最噁畢業演說，小粉紅裝腔作勢大外宣！美籍華裔學生完勝蔣雨融...
item.itunes:author: 公子沈 Mr. Shen
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 這份摘要濃縮了優秀學生代表 Andrew Yang 的畢業典禮演講精華。
  • 演講內容不僅分享了學術成就，更深入探討了人際互動、社群建立以及擁抱不確定性的重要性。
  • 藉由科學類比（如波紋與互動），生動闡釋了如何從日常體驗中汲取人生智慧。
  • 展現了畢業生群體如何克服困難、相互支持，並對未來充滿信心。

摘要

得獎榮譽與傑出成就

影片首先介紹了榮獲多項最高榮譽的畢業生代表 Andrew Yang。他是物理學與應用數學的雙主修學生，並在材料科學、應用物理及應用數學等多個領域展現卓越才能。他的研究方向廣泛，從神經網路到利用高速繞射數據分析材料結構的變化。Andrew Yang 將繼續在加州理工學院深造，進行中子繞射與機器學習相關研究。

謙遜開場與多元群體

Andrew Yang 謙遜地開場，自嘲是一個帶著《Among Us》鑰匙圈的「書呆子」，與其他同學在清潔能源、航太科技等領域的傑出成就形成對比。他分享了擔任助教的經驗，並強調哥倫比亞大學學生雖然面對相同教材，卻展現出多元的互動方式，這也反映了整個班級的多元群體特質。他提到同學來自全美五十州、近半數國家，許多人更是家中第一位大學畢業生。

共同經歷與社群連結

儘管背景各異，同學們共同經歷了大學生活中的種種挑戰，包括艱難的入門物理課程、宿舍酷熱、通宵趕工專案，以及長時間在圖書館學習。Andrew Yang 藉由傅立葉的理論，將這些日常的互動——從初識的揮手到畢業的道別——比喻為結合形成美麗函數的「波」，強調是這些共同經歷與互動，建構了他們這個美麗的社群共同體。

科學類比與人生波紋

Andrew Yang 巧妙地將自己的研究領域——用雷射光研究奈米尺度材料結構——與人生體驗連結。他解釋說，就像雷射光在不完美的鏡面上會產生微小的波紋，這些「最微小的波」能揭示材料的原子組成和缺陷。同樣地，人生中的微小互動和經歷，構成了我們這個社群共同體的波紋，每個波紋都至關重要。

人文關懷與核心價值

演講強調，除了嚴謹的課程和研究機會，真正定義哥倫比亞大學工程學院學生的，是對彼此的承諾，這體現於「工程學為了人類」的校訓。Andrew Yang 分享了一個在洛杉磯迷路時與陌生人（一位前社會學教授）進行深刻哲學辯論的經歷，證明了他們的人文關懷教育的價值。

擁抱不確定性：冒險的重要性

Andrew Yang 的最後一個重點是冒險的重要性，即「在不確定性的波紋中濺水前行」。他坦承有些風險帶來了不愉快的後果（如在日本被拘留、喝溪水生病），但更多的冒險則開啟了令人驚豔的機會，例如跨越太平洋探索物理學、成為研討會第一位本科生演講者，以及穿著電玩服裝發表畢業演說。

面對挑戰與展望未來

儘管當前世界讓冒險變得更加困難，Andrew Yang 對班級克服困難的能力充滿信心。他回顧了同學們共同面對的挑戰，包括校園淹水、疫情大流行，甚至是在沒有生成式 AI 的年代完成學業。他總結說，經過風雨洗禮，他們終於抵達了大學生涯的頂峰，並引用雙關語鼓勵大家：「我們是 C 班的（或哥倫比亞大學的 C），我們將會製造浪潮（make waves）。

================================================

painful moments last summer with a brain. cancer. diagnosis. Dad, you've beaten the odds. and I'm so thankful that you're here. today. The confidence I've grown into. this year hasn't come from success. It's. come from surviving this hardship and. failure. Many times I felt down. I was. actually on my way. up. So in these first postgrad years,. you're going to be tempted to compare. your success to others. But fermentation. takes time. So will your. success. I hope you're lucky enough to. not always succeed immediately, lucky. enough to sometimes fail, and wise. enough to wait for the process to. finish. The process stinks. Sometimes it. feels like you're going to run in. circles for no reason at all, but that's. when real real progress happens. I did. track and field. You can trust. me. Be patient. Life's a dream, but. sometimes it's first a. pickle. Lesson. three. Bridge. Bridge is a card game that I've. played every Sunday since coming to. Ammerst. I've played with a lot of. seniors and not just my classmates. because when I say I play bridge, people. usually go, "Oh, my grandma plays. that." So, how did I get involved with a. hobby of the Margarets and Gertudes of. the. world? I started the way many college. activities do, hanging out with your. friends. Unlike most activities, I got. involved because Big Mike bribed me. during orientation week. He said I could get paid to learn a. dying card game. So on Sunday, I showed. up to North Common Room at 4 p.m. No. questions asked. Now, I hated Bridge at. first. It's a tough game to learn. You. sit at a table and play as a partnership. with the person across from you. Lots of. rules, lots of teamwork, and lots of. frustration. But when the $15 hit my Venmo the next. day, unemployed freshman me. reconsidered. So my friends and I kept. showing up week after week to get paid. and learn how to play bridge. Before. long, we loved the card game itself,. founded the Amoris Bridge Club, and. began traveling to tournaments across. the world, meeting tons of new people. Full disclosure, we don't pay you to. play bridge anymore. So, I know it's a. tough cell, but even if you don't start. playing bridge, I hope you'll remember. bridges. Because the unexpected gift of. bridge has been the connections it's. built me. Bridges that span generations. and. geographies. Bridge isn't just a card. game. It's about partnership. We share. strategies with each other, yes, but. also stories and life lessons. Each. table I've played at is a new. conversation and lets me glimpse. different life paths. I've played with. Jeff, a 40-year-old Canadian teacher,.

Zeia, an 80-year-old Pakistani. Grandmaster, Bill, a retired biologist,. and even Bill. Gates. Many friendships I've made. through Bridge wouldn't have existed if. I didn't seek out this connection. So. find mentors, learn who to trust,. because not all advice is worth. taking. Not all advice is worth taking. If you don't believe me, you're already. doing a great. job. No one does life alone, so lean on. our Ammeris. community. Some of my earliest life. advice came from alums who I reached out. to. Mike Reed, class of '04. AJ Chiansa. and Ryan Park05, Harran Tong, 2023, and. many, many. more. These alums responded to emails. from some green freshman who knew. nothing about a career in law, and they. spent hours conversing with me. They. built me a. bridge. Bridges go two ways. What bridge. will you build for someone else? In just a few minutes, you'll also be an. alum who can start building bridges for. others. Take some time to mentor the. interns at work or respond to that. amorous freshman who emails you because. you never know, you might get a shout. out at their. commencement. Look to receive and create. bridges. For me, it started with a silly. little card game, bridge. Find your. bridge and pay it. forward. It's finally time I give my. stolen box. back. But before I do, seniors, take a. moment to ask yourself, what will you. take away from your time at Ammerst? And. what will you give back to others in. life after. Ammerst? It might take a while to answer. that. So remember, Ammerst isn't going. to be just one time or place in our. lives. It's going to be an eternal. community of friends and a lifelong of. explaining that no, we're different from. UMass. Ammerst. When our parents first sent us. here, I'm sure that they hoped we'd. become less naive and maybe figure out a. life plan. But I hope we leave just as. green and still a little uncertain about. our road ahead. Because as the poet who we named our. kind of ugly Frost Library after once. said, "Two roads diverged. I chose the. one less traveled by, and that has made. all the. difference." I hope you choose the roads. not. taken. I hope you hold on to your. greenness and the things many others. would leave behind because some of those. things, like green to-go boxes, are. actually worth keeping around. So before I end, I want to thank. President Elliot, the board of trustees,. professors, and all members of the. college. You've truly made Ammerst. special, and it's been an honor to speak. today. Thank you families for coming all. this way. While other commencements get. Nobel Prize winners and Kermit the Frog,. you got. me finally. Thank you and. congratulations. seniors. It was a pleasure to be young. with you. As we depart, let's keep these. three green box lessons in mind. Build.

bridges, embrace the rotten, and bring. our freshman greenness to go.

Andrew Yang 謙遜地開場，自嘲是一個帶著《Among Us》鑰匙圈的「書呆子」，與其他同學在清潔能源、航太科技等領域的傑出成就形成對比。他分享了擔任助教的經驗，並強調哥倫比亞大學學生雖然面對相同教材，卻展現出多元的互動方式，這也反映了整個班級的多元群體特質。他提到同學來自全美五十州、近半數國家，許多人更是家中第一位大學畢業生。

共同經歷與社群連結

儘管背景各異，同學們共同經歷了大學生活中的種種挑戰，包括艱難的入門物理課程、宿舍酷熱、通宵趕工專案，以及長時間在圖書館學習。Andrew Yang 藉由傅立葉的理論，將這些日常的互動——從初識的揮手到畢業的道別——比喻為結合形成美麗函數的「波」，強調是這些共同經歷與互動，建構了他們這個美麗的社群共同體。

科學類比與人生波紋

Andrew Yang 巧妙地將自己的研究領域——用雷射光研究奈米尺度材料結構——與人生體驗連結。他解釋說，就像雷射光在不完美的鏡面上會產生微小的波紋，這些「最微小的波」能揭示材料的原子組成和缺陷。同樣地，人生中的微小互動和經歷，構成了我們這個社群共同體的波紋，每個波紋都至關重要。

人文關懷與核心價值

演講強調，除了嚴謹的課程和研究機會，真正定義哥倫比亞大學工程學院學生的，是對彼此的承諾，這體現於「工程學為了人類」的校訓。Andrew Yang 分享了一個在洛杉磯迷路時與陌生人（一位前社會學教授）進行深刻哲學辯論的經歷，證明了他們的人文關懷教育的價值。

擁抱不確定性：冒險的重要性

Andrew Yang 的最後一個重點是冒險的重要性，即「在不確定性的波紋中濺水前行」。他坦承有些風險帶來了不愉快的後果（如在日本被拘留、喝溪水生病），但更多的冒險則開啟了令人驚豔的機會，例如跨越太平洋探索物理學、成為研討會第一位本科生演講者，以及穿著電玩服裝發表畢業演說。

面對挑戰與展望未來

儘管當前世界讓冒險變得更加困難，Andrew Yang 對班級克服困難的能力充滿信心。他回顧了同學們共同面對的挑戰，包括校園淹水、疫情大流行，甚至是在沒有生成式 AI 的年代完成學業。他總結說，經過風雨洗禮，他們終於抵達了大學生涯的頂峰，並引用雙關語鼓勵大家：「我們是 C 班的（或哥倫比亞大學的 C），我們將會製造浪潮（make waves）。

================================================

But when the $15 hit my Venmo the next. day, unemployed freshman me. reconsidered. So my friends and I kept. showing up week after week to get paid. and learn how to play bridge. Before. long, we loved the card game itself,. founded the Amoris Bridge Club, and. began traveling to tournaments across. the world, meeting tons of new people. We share. strategies with each other, yes, but. also stories and life lessons. Each. table I've played at is a new. conversation and lets me glimpse. different life paths. I've played with. Jeff, a 40-year-old Canadian teacher,.

Zeia, an 80-year-old Pakistani. Grandmaster, Bill, a retired biologist,. and even Bill. Gates. Many friendships I've made. through Bridge wouldn't have existed if. I didn't seek out this connection. So. find mentors, learn who to trust,. because not all advice is worth. taking. Not all advice is worth taking. If you don't believe me, you're already. doing a great. job. No one does life alone, so lean on. our Ammeris. community. Some of my earliest life. advice came from alums who I reached out. to. Mike Reed, class of '04. AJ Chiansa. and Ryan Park05, Harran Tong, 2023, and. many, many. more. What bridge. will you build for someone else? In just a few minutes, you'll also be an. alum who can start building bridges for. others. Take some time to mentor the. interns at work or respond to that. amorous freshman who emails you because. you never know, you might get a shout. out at their. commencement. Look to receive and create. bridges. For me, it started with a silly. little card game, bridge. Find your. bridge and pay it. forward. It's finally time I give my. stolen box. back. But before I do, seniors, take a. moment to ask yourself, what will you. take away from your time at Ammerst? And. what will you give back to others in. life after. Ammerst? It might take a while to answer. that. So remember, Ammerst isn't going. to be just one time or place in our. lives. It's going to be an eternal. community of friends and a lifelong of. explaining that no, we're different from. UMass. Ammerst. When our parents first sent us. here, I'm sure that they hoped we'd. become less naive and maybe figure out a. life plan. But I hope we leave just as. green and still a little uncertain about. our road ahead. Because as the poet who we named our. kind of ugly Frost Library after once. said, "Two roads diverged. I chose the. one less traveled by, and that has made. all the. difference." I hope you choose the roads. not. taken. I hope you hold on to your. greenness and the things many others. would leave behind because some of those. things, like green to-go boxes, are. actually worth keeping around. So before I end, I want to thank. President Elliot, the board of trustees,. professors, and all members of the. college. You've truly made Ammerst. special, and it's been an honor to speak. today. Thank you families for coming all. this way. While other commencements get. Nobel Prize winners and Kermit the Frog,. you got. me finally. Thank you and. congratulations. seniors. It was a pleasure to be young. with you. As we depart, let's keep these. three green box lessons in mind. Build.

bridges, embrace the rotten, and bring. our freshman greenness to go.

item.content:encoded:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 這份摘要濃縮了優秀學生代表 Andrew Yang 的畢業典禮演講精華。
  • 演講內容不僅分享了學術成就，更深入探討了人際互動、社群建立以及擁抱不確定性的重要性。
  • 藉由科學類比（如波紋與互動），生動闡釋了如何從日常體驗中汲取人生智慧。
  • 展現了畢業生群體如何克服困難、相互支持，並對未來充滿信心。

摘要

得獎榮譽與傑出成就

影片首先介紹了榮獲多項最高榮譽的畢業生代表 Andrew Yang。他是物理學與應用數學的雙主修學生，並在材料科學、應用物理及應用數學等多個領域展現卓越才能。他的研究方向廣泛，從神經網路到利用高速繞射數據分析材料結構的變化。Andrew Yang 將繼續在加州理工學院深造，進行中子繞射與機器學習相關研究。

謙遜開場與多元群體

Andrew Yang 謙遜地開場，自嘲是一個帶著《Among Us》鑰匙圈的「書呆子」，與其他同學在清潔能源、航太科技等領域的傑出成就形成對比。他分享了擔任助教的經驗，並強調哥倫比亞大學學生雖然面對相同教材，卻展現出多元的互動方式，這也反映了整個班級的多元群體特質。他提到同學來自全美五十州、近半數國家，許多人更是家中第一位大學畢業生。

共同經歷與社群連結

儘管背景各異，同學們共同經歷了大學生活中的種種挑戰，包括艱難的入門物理課程、宿舍酷熱、通宵趕工專案，以及長時間在圖書館學習。Andrew Yang 藉由傅立葉的理論，將這些日常的互動——從初識的揮手到畢業的道別——比喻為結合形成美麗函數的「波」，強調是這些共同經歷與互動，建構了他們這個美麗的社群共同體。

科學類比與人生波紋

Andrew Yang 巧妙地將自己的研究領域——用雷射光研究奈米尺度材料結構——與人生體驗連結。他解釋說，就像雷射光在不完美的鏡面上會產生微小的波紋，這些「最微小的波」能揭示材料的原子組成和缺陷。同樣地，人生中的微小互動和經歷，構成了我們這個社群共同體的波紋，每個波紋都至關重要。

人文關懷與核心價值

演講強調，除了嚴謹的課程和研究機會，真正定義哥倫比亞大學工程學院學生的，是對彼此的承諾，這體現於「工程學為了人類」的校訓。Andrew Yang 分享了一個在洛杉磯迷路時與陌生人（一位前社會學教授）進行深刻哲學辯論的經歷，證明了他們的人文關懷教育的價值。

擁抱不確定性：冒險的重要性

Andrew Yang 的最後一個重點是冒險的重要性，即「在不確定性的波紋中濺水前行」。他坦承有些風險帶來了不愉快的後果（如在日本被拘留、喝溪水生病），但更多的冒險則開啟了令人驚豔的機會，例如跨越太平洋探索物理學、成為研討會第一位本科生演講者，以及穿著電玩服裝發表畢業演說。

面對挑戰與展望未來

儘管當前世界讓冒險變得更加困難，Andrew Yang 對班級克服困難的能力充滿信心。他回顧了同學們共同面對的挑戰，包括校園淹水、疫情大流行，甚至是在沒有生成式 AI 的年代完成學業。他總結說，經過風雨洗禮，他們終於抵達了大學生涯的頂峰，並引用雙關語鼓勵大家：「我們是 C 班的（或哥倫比亞大學的 C），我們將會製造浪潮（make waves）。

So on Sunday, I showed. up to North Common Room at 4 p.m. No. questions asked. Now, I hated Bridge at. first. It's a tough game to learn. Before. long, we loved the card game itself,. founded the Amoris Bridge Club, and. began traveling to tournaments across. the world, meeting tons of new people. Because the unexpected gift of. bridge has been the connections it's. built me. Bridges that span generations. and. geographies. Bridge isn't just a card. game. It's about partnership. We share. strategies with each other, yes, but. also stories and life lessons. Each. table I've played at is a new. conversation and lets me glimpse. different life paths. I've played with. Jeff, a 40-year-old Canadian teacher,.

Zeia, an 80-year-old Pakistani. Grandmaster, Bill, a retired biologist,. and even Bill. Gates. Many friendships I've made. through Bridge wouldn't have existed if. I didn't seek out this connection. So. find mentors, learn who to trust,. because not all advice is worth. taking. Not all advice is worth taking. If you don't believe me, you're already. doing a great. job. No one does life alone, so lean on. our Ammeris. community. Some of my earliest life. advice came from alums who I reached out. to. Mike Reed, class of '04. AJ Chiansa. and Ryan Park05, Harran Tong, 2023, and. many, many. more. What bridge. will you build for someone else? In just a few minutes, you'll also be an. alum who can start building bridges for. others. Take some time to mentor the. interns at work or respond to that. amorous freshman who emails you because. you never know, you might get a shout. out at their. commencement. Look to receive and create. bridges. For me, it started with a silly. little card game, bridge. Find your. bridge and pay it. forward. It's finally time I give my. stolen box. back. But before I do, seniors, take a. moment to ask yourself, what will you. take away from your time at Ammerst? And. what will you give back to others in. life after. Ammerst? It might take a while to answer. that. So remember, Ammerst isn't going. to be just one time or place in our. lives. It's going to be an eternal. community of friends and a lifelong of. explaining that no, we're different from. UMass. Ammerst. When our parents first sent us. here, I'm sure that they hoped we'd. become less naive and maybe figure out a. life plan. But I hope we leave just as. green and still a little uncertain about. our road ahead. Because as the poet who we named our. kind of ugly Frost Library after once. said, "Two roads diverged. I chose the. one less traveled by, and that has made. all the. difference." I hope you choose the roads. not. taken. item.itunes:title: 中國妥協解禁稀土，邀川普訪華求放過！美國制裁還在加碼？德國宣布和中國脫鉤，中共的日子不太好過...
item.itunes:author: 公子沈 Mr. Shen
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 了解近期美中互動（如川習通話）的真實意義，超越表面報導。
  • 掌握美國對中國持續加碼制裁以及中國讓步的具體情況和原因。
  • 深入分析美中關係惡化的根本原因，以及中國內部政策如何影響其國際形象。

摘要

在最新的《公子時評》節目中，作者 公子沈 探討了近期似乎出現的美中關係「回暖」跡象，特別是川普與習近平的通話。根據報導，習近平主動致電川普，雙方談及關稅和貿易戰，習近平承諾中國不再限制稀土出口，並邀請川普訪華，川普也表示歡迎習近平訪美。表面上，這似乎預示著未來可能的互訪和關係改善。

作者對回暖的質疑

然而，作者對此表示悲觀，認為這並不代表美中關係真正回暖。作者回顧了川普第一任期訪問中國後，美中貿易戰反而正式開啟，美國的國防戰略也開始將中國定位為潛在對手，並啟動了科技封鎖。作者指出，儘管川普在言論上偶爾對習近平和中國多有好話，但其落實的政策完全符合美國兩黨共識，並非總統一人能決定，美國的內政外交受到智囊、內閣及國會的牽制與監督。

中國的讓步與美國的加碼

作者認為，中國在稀土出口上的讓步是受到美國壓力的結果，例如美國限制了中國大飛機所需的發動機技術出口，這被視為對中國限制稀土出口的報復。儘管中國解禁稀土，但美國在其他領域的制裁仍在加碼，例如不允許向中國出口乙烷（被認為會增強中國軍事實力），並暫停向中國出口核電站零部件和設備。中國發言人對此的回應（要求美方取消負面措施）被作者解讀為一種「跪地求饒」。

美中關係惡化的深層原因與未來走向

作者分析，美國之所以有這種強硬姿態，是因為中國的崛起建立在全球化基礎上，而全球化由美國主導。中國對西方技術輸入的依賴遠超西方對中國製造和市場的依賴，西方供應鏈已開始「去風險化」。此外，諸如中國留學生涉嫌走私真菌等事件，以及中國官方媒體宣傳仇視日本（如電影《731》預告片中的言論「天堂不會再有日本人」）等行為，不斷損害中國在美國乃至西方世界的民意基礎，讓中國的「大外宣」努力失效。這些事件表明，中國政府及其政策對美國的國家安全和利益構成威脅，導致美中關係的敵意不斷加深，與種族歧視無關，而是與中共的作為有關。

作者總結，當前世界已進入「你死我活」的對抗狀態，是東西方兩大陣營的對壘，勝負取決於實力而非談判。儘管過程中西方世界可能也會受到衝擊，但作者對最終的勝利抱持信心，並強調當前的衝突是我們這個時代必然會經歷的。

=============

在最新的《公子時評》節目中，作者 公子沈 探討了近期似乎出現的美中關係「回暖」跡象，特別是川普與習近平的通話。根據報導，習近平主動致電川普，雙方談及關稅和貿易戰，習近平承諾中國不再限制稀土出口，並邀請川普訪華，川普也表示歡迎習近平訪美。表面上，這似乎預示著未來可能的互訪和關係改善。

作者對回暖的質疑

然而，作者對此表示悲觀，認為這並不代表美中關係真正回暖。作者回顧了川普第一任期訪問中國後，美中貿易戰反而正式開啟，美國的國防戰略也開始將中國定位為潛在對手，並啟動了科技封鎖。作者指出，儘管川普在言論上偶爾對習近平和中國多有好話，但其落實的政策完全符合美國兩黨共識，並非總統一人能決定，美國的內政外交受到智囊、內閣及國會的牽制與監督。

中國的讓步與美國的加碼

作者認為，中國在稀土出口上的讓步是受到美國壓力的結果，例如美國限制了中國大飛機所需的發動機技術出口，這被視為對中國限制稀土出口的報復。儘管中國解禁稀土，但美國在其他領域的制裁仍在加碼，例如不允許向中國出口乙烷（被認為會增強中國軍事實力），並暫停向中國出口核電站零部件和設備。中國發言人對此的回應（要求美方取消負面措施）被作者解讀為一種「跪地求饒」。

美中關係惡化的深層原因與未來走向

作者分析，美國之所以有這種強硬姿態，是因為中國的崛起建立在全球化基礎上，而全球化由美國主導。中國對西方技術輸入的依賴遠超西方對中國製造和市場的依賴，西方供應鏈已開始「去風險化」。此外，諸如中國留學生涉嫌走私真菌等事件，以及中國官方媒體宣傳仇視日本（如電影《731》預告片中的言論「天堂不會再有日本人」）等行為，不斷損害中國在美國乃至西方世界的民意基礎，讓中國的「大外宣」努力失效。這些事件表明，中國政府及其政策對美國的國家安全和利益構成威脅，導致美中關係的敵意不斷加深，與種族歧視無關，而是與中共的作為有關。

作者總結，當前世界已進入「你死我活」的對抗狀態，是東西方兩大陣營的對壘，勝負取決於實力而非談判。儘管過程中西方世界可能也會受到衝擊，但作者對最終的勝利抱持信心，並強調當前的衝突是我們這個時代必然會經歷的。

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=S7jTieIBADs

item.content:encoded:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 了解近期美中互動（如川習通話）的真實意義，超越表面報導。
  • 掌握美國對中國持續加碼制裁以及中國讓步的具體情況和原因。
  • 深入分析美中關係惡化的根本原因，以及中國內部政策如何影響其國際形象。

item.title: 不裝，說人話！台灣裔導演朱浩偉南加大致詞，完勝哈佛大學中國小粉紅蔣雨融！【公子分享】
item.itunes:title: 不裝，說人話！台灣裔導演朱浩偉南加大致詞，完勝哈佛大學中國小粉紅蔣雨融！【公子分享】
item.itunes:author: 公子沈 Mr. Shen
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 了解一位知名導演如何將個人成長故事，從初戀失利到找到人生熱情，轉化為對應屆畢業生的深刻啟示。
  • 洞悉在充滿不確定性（如疫情、經濟危機、環境災難）的時代，新世代所面臨的獨特挑戰與改造世界的機會。
  • 學習「說故事的力量」如何超越知識傳遞，能引發同理心、促成行動，並幫助人們重新定義自我與周遭世界。

摘要

演講者Chu博士回到母校體育場，這個地方對他意義深重，既有美好的回憶，也曾是他情感受挫的見證。他憶起當年高中女友在他入學一年後也考進同一所學校，原本期待能一起「稱霸」校園，沒想到第一天就被甩，女友還和明星美式足球員交往。這讓他一度難以踏足校園甚至不敢來看比賽。然而，當那位明星球員在一次關鍵比賽中失手錯失致勝球時，身為校友的他雖感遺憾，但18歲不成熟的他卻為此度過了「人生中最美好的一週」。這個有點複雜的開場，讓聽眾感受到他的真誠與幽默。

Chu博士接著分享他如今的家庭，他擁有一位傑出的太太克莉絲汀和五個年幼的孩子，笑稱太太「幸好」對運動員沒興趣，反而喜歡他這種有點中年發福的亞裔男性，幽默地將自己的「老爹身材」歸功於大學時期對碳水化合物的熱愛。他對能帶家人回到南加州大學（USC）感到興奮，因為校園的每一處都承載著他成長的記憶，那些塑造了今天他的點點滴滴。從宿舍生活到發現自己對酒精過敏，再到對碳水化合物的熱愛，甚至是偷偷溜進奧斯卡頒獎典禮，這些經歷都構築了他的一部分。他也特別提到他學生時期短片攝影師，在二十二年後成為他最新電影《威卡》（Wicked）的攝影指導，強調了長久的連結。

他對眼前充滿活力的畢業生們表達了祝賀，提醒他們此刻不僅是他們自己成功，也是他們身邊那些了解他們所有缺點、所有過錯，卻依然愛著他們的人們的成功。Chu博士承認，在準備這次演講時，他深入思考了這一代畢業生所經歷的一切：新冠疫情、金融危機、環境災難等等。儘管如此，他們依然堅持下來，完成了學業，這讓他和所有人都為之驕傲。

然而，他認為這些畢業生即將面對更為艱鉅的任務。身為一個說故事的人，他起初不確定自己能給予什麼建議，甚至開玩笑說學校應該找一位世界領袖或發明家來演講。但越是思考，他越是覺得自己人生中的某些經驗或許能有所幫助，因為「我們的世界正在碰撞」。無論未來從事何種行業，未來都是相互連結的，並且需要「真實地面對」，以便做好準備。他相信這一代人面臨著前所未有的深刻任務，不僅僅是繼承一個變動的世界，更要從根本上定義我們將成為誰，去重新想像世界，並為未來設定基礎。他形容我們正處於一個「舊故事正在崩塌」的時刻，恐懼、指責和分裂似乎佔據主導，但這同時也是一個深刻的「機會」，因為當舊故事瓦解，正是時候書寫一個新故事。

Chu博士分享了他的「故事」的開端：加州洛斯阿圖斯一家名為「主廚周」的中國餐廳。他的父母是來自台灣的移民，於1969年創業並經營至今，以舒適的價格提供家常菜。他形容父母是「美國夢」的活生生體現，儘管剛來美國時英語有限，但他們熱愛美國文化，並渴望孩子們能融入社會。他的父母將「奧茲巫師」視為不朽的美國童話，教導他們只要遵循黃磚路—信念、紀律、勤奮工作並熱愛所做之事—就能實現夢想。在餐廳裡，父母教導他們要成為文化的「大使」，不僅要滿足顧客的胃，更要贏得尊重，改變人們對華裔家庭的看法。

每年奧斯卡頒獎典禮是家中的盛事，他們會圍坐電視前觀看，對那些好萊塢名人充滿敬畏與嚮往。然而，從餐廳的餐桌到好萊塢，似乎是一條遙不可及的路，一個沒有任何背景、看起來不像他們家人能進入的世界。直到有一天，他找到了一個「鏡頭」：一台小型的Sony攝影機。當他透過觀景窗看世界時，周遭的一切都黯淡下來，只剩下一個小小的光框。他那過度活躍的思緒平靜下來，時間彷彿變慢，他「屈服於當下」，並透過這個框架發現了那些日常生活中原本看不到的「瞬間」和「細節」。

這個發現就像他的「秘密」，但當他在電視上播放錄像時，這個秘密彷彿可以與家人共享。即使家人會笑著抱怨鏡頭晃動，但他們是「與他同在」的。他發現這台機器正在「召喚同理心」。他用攝影機編輯了一個家庭旅遊影片，加上音樂後在客廳播放，家人們看著螢幕上的自己，眼睛被緊緊吸引。幾分鐘後，他們流下了眼淚。他們在螢幕上看到了那個「實現美國夢」的家庭，他們的夢想以畫面形式傳承給下一代。透過剪輯，他讓他們對未來的一切希望變得「真實可信」。

這個經歷讓他著迷。更重要的是，他們不僅僅是看到了螢幕上的家庭，他們「也變成了」螢幕上的那個家庭。儘管旅行的細節他們早已知曉，但真正讓他們至今仍津津樂道的是觀看影片時的「感受」。螢幕上的一個畫面，賦予了他們的家庭「價值」，為他們往後的日子設定了敘事。這是一個「啟示」：一個隨機的片段可以組織成一個故事，引發情感反應，產生同理心，進而促使觀看者採取行動。在今天這個「知識唾手可得」的世界裡，這點尤其重要，也是他今晚極力想傳達給畢業生的訊息。

================================================

But the. t but the timeless American fairy tale. that played in our household was Wizard. of Oz. And my parent Yes. My parents. instilled in us that if we just follow. the yellow brick road, faith,. discipline, hard work, and loved what we. did, then the American dream would lead. us to the wizard who would grant us our. heart's desire. And as the ultimate. hosts at their restaurant, my parents. loved being part of the American fabric. John, we might be the first Chinese. family these customers have ever met. We. must not just fill their bellies, but. fill their hearts so that the next time. they meet a Chinese family, they will. give them more respect. We're. ambassadors, they'd say. Okay, Mom. Whatever the pinnacle of the year would. be when we'd gather around the TV and. watch the Academy Awards, the Oscars. These Hollywood people were beautiful. cultural leaders, fashion icons,. untouchable to normal human beings. And. when they spoke, the world stood in. applause. Oftentimes, my parents would. actually applaud right in the living. room as well. But to actually get to. Hollywood, that was a far far cry from. the restaurant booth I do my homework. in. We had zero connections into this. world. And to me, the gates were so. high, it didn't look like they let. anyone in, let alone anyone who looked. like my family. And then one day, I. found the lens in the form of a little. sony camcorder, and it changed me. The. moment I looked into the viewfinder,. that little tubular eyepiece you put. your eye against to see what the lens. sees, everything around my vision dimmed. to darkness, and only a small frame of. light appeared. My hyper mind became. calm and silent. Time slowed down. A. surrender to the present. And through. this little frame of light, I found. moments. Moments that I couldn't see. otherwise. Details about life. My dad. grabbing the hand of my mother, my. brother's long legs leaping from the. stairs, a close-up of my sister's eyes. opening uh wide while she watched my. other sister open a birthday gift. And. it felt like my secret. But when I would. play back on the TV, it felt like I. could share my secret as if they were. right there within within my own eyes. And they would actually yell at the. screen at me as if I they were right. there. They're saying, "Move the camera. down, John. Stop moving. You're giving. us a headache." But they were with me. This thing was conjuring empathy. I had. never experienced a tool as powerful as. this. It just kept making me more. curious at what else I could do. So I. edited a family vacation video together. with music and sats down in the living. room to watch. The moment they saw us on. the bright screen, their eyes locked in. A few moments. later, they teared up. They saw our. family as the American dream fulfilled. Their dream passed on to us now on the. screen. Cut to music like a Jean Kelly. musical. Everything they had hoped for,. I could make believe existed. And I was. hooked. Interestingly, we didn't just. see that family on the TV. We also.

became that family on the TV. My parents. already knew the information. We went on. the vacation. We did all the things in. the itinerary. But what makes them still. talk about that vacation today was what. they felt when they watched that video. One image of us gave us worth that set. the narrative for who we are for the. rest of our lives. This was a. revelation. A random clip can turn into. an organized story that can turn into an. emotional reaction that causes empathy. that can then cause action on the part. of the viewer. This is even more. important in today's world and something. that really compelled me to communicate. to you this evening. Because while. knowledge is now accessible to anyone. with AI within seconds, gaining. understanding and empathy is more. elusive and now more valuable. And some. already know. this. Some already know this and it's. become a business today. Storytelling is. both our greatest hope and our biggest. battleground. Everyone from marketers to. politicians to AI itself is competing. for control over narratives. Whoever. tells the best story holds the power. Thus, your ability to understand,. interpret, and ultimately shape stories. is critically important no matter what. you want to do or how you want to live. your life. When I started making films. in high school, I was the only video guy. on the campus. Now every kid in America. has a phone and knows how to edit. And. that's a big change with big. consequences. It's not enough to. passively consume content anymore. We. must become active interpreters and. thoughtful. creators. We must have enough story. grammar to discern truth from falsehood,. authenticity from manipulation, and real. empathy from performative emotions. Machines can analyze data, predict. patterns, even create art, but they. cannot authentically feel or intuitively. connect. In whatever field you are in,. your power to convey information in ways. that emotionally connect will be more. valuable than we even currently. acknowledge. So, I urge you to embrace. your understanding of story even beyond. your comfort zone because I believe it's. the only way the groundbreaking ideas. you plan to bring to this world will. truly land and endure. I know you are. graduating. You know who you are. You. mastered your major and you are on the. verge of becoming the answer to the. age-old question, what do you want to be. when you grow up? Well, I recommend you. reject that question completely. It. feels like Thank you. That guy. definitely rejected that. question. It feels like a final. definitive form, but the very nature of. growth is. change. What do you want to be while. you're growing? Now, that's a question I. think if you're doing it right every. day, you're becoming more finely tuned. to the truth of who you are. Like. musicians in a symphony, you'll find. your life's themes recurring in new. keys, old wounds transforming into new. gifts, and relationships. relationships. shifting and deepening over time or. falling apart. And that's okay. That is. not a mistake. That's a work in process. Your job isn't to solve yourself. It is. to learn to play your notes with. increasing honesty and complexity. You. will change sooner and more than you can. even imagine right now. And so will your. closest friends. So give them grace to.

do it. And I hope they give you the. same. And coming out of college, when I. was sitting where you are tonight, I was. terrified about where to start. But what. I did know was that I didn't want to. talk about me being. Asian. I had spent my whole life just. trying to fit in so my friends wouldn't. make Asian jokes or that the only film. sent to me wouldn't be ones about China. I didn't want to be an Asian filmmaker. I just wanted to be a filmmaker. But I. heard a quote, "Worry is the misuse of. imagination." And it rocked me. Worry is the misuse of imagination. We. think so deeply about what can go wrong,. we leave no room for the imagination of. what happens when it all goes right. And. if we can redirect that imagination. towards creation, we stop merely. performing inherited scripts and start. authoring our own. And your true legacy. isn't something you're going to leave. behind. It's the harmful pattern you. recognize and change. Or as Steve Jobs. would say, the dent in the universe. That whirlwind of emotions was where I. was at when the movie Crazy Rich Asians. came into my life. A movie I made to. comfort the scariest to confront the. scariest thing I have ever known. My own. cultural identity crisis. I wanted to. make a movie about Asian characters that. were beautiful, hilarious,. materialistic, quirky, ambitious, sexy,. powerful in all the ways, flaws and all. And that American movies had never p uh. painted us before. The data folks in. Hollywood saw no upside, no audience. history for a romcom led by the Asian. diaspora. So I used my storytelling. pitching skills to convince them to do. it. And did I know things would change? No. But my imagination was already. spinning with the possibility, not the. worry. And personally, I had felt the. urgent or I had felt um the urgent pull. to do this at about the age of 38. After. I met my wife, all my priorities. changed. But also, we were about to have. our first child, my daughter, Willow,. who's also here. tonight. And I I had to turn the. viewfinder back onto myself and ask the. question, when I look through that lens,. am I proud of who I see? What was I. still hiding as we were ushering a new. life into this world? I wanted her to be. proud of her heritage, her skin, her. hair, to know how beautiful every part. of her is. And yet was. I. And I wanted to know the long line of. kick-ass women that came before her,. that was in her blood. My Anna, my boo. boo, my po, my mom, and my wife. Truth. Truthfully, having made many movie. sequels up to that point, I had lost my. optimism. I felt like I wasn't becoming. the filmmaker I always thought I was. capable of. I felt like no one wanted to. listen to what I actually was. So, it. made me feel like an impostor. But. somehow I look back at that American. dream story that my parents believed in. so deeply. That Wizard of Oz fairy tale. of the yellow brick road and the wizard. waiting for us. And it dawned on me. The. story has. changed. And I wanted better for my. daughter. Even though I had done. everything I was asked to do, maybe the. yellow brick road was never made for us. in mind. Maybe there was no man behind. the curtain waiting to help us. Maybe. what we thought was home never thought. we even lived here. Suddenly, memories I. had forgotten since I was a kid came. rushing back. Go back to your country. I.

remember being yelled at outside the. Tower Records. Don't forget to stick. with your race was whispered to me by a. teacher. I used to laugh that off and. I'm honestly glad I did because I don't. think I could have processed it. And. suddenly it was all crashing in on me. And I felt compelled to make a story. about someone who was Asian but all. American going to Asia for the first. time to confront these feelings of. inadequacy and country. And rather than. financial worth, it was going to be. about self-worth and finding one's. cultural generational center in a world. of new rules defined by the generation. itself. A new generation could still. self-love and self-care, but sacrifice. it all for another if we chose to. We. still have the guts and determination of. our ancestors, even if we order from. Uber. Eats. I mean, I already signed up for. the membership, so I have to order from. there. One of my longtime producers said. to me while I was shooting, "I can see. the dragon rising in you." And that made. me smile because deep down I knew it was. true. I was becoming a father. And that. meant getting my stories. straight. Two weeks ago, two weeks after. finishing the shoot for Crazy Rich. Asian, Willow was born. Stevie Wonder. played. Yeah. Ste Stevie Wonder played. the moment she took her first breath. It. feels like this is the beginning. Though. I've known you for a million years, he. sings. No lyrics could be more true. For. Crazy Rich Asians, we resisted going. with a streaming company because we felt. like putting this movie on the big. screen would tell a story in itself. That the people that look like this cast. are worth your time and money to buy a. ticket to a theater. Something Hollywood. hadn't quite done yet. And we felt like it's not just the. movie, but the campaign itself could. tell that story. And if the movie was. actually funny and romantic and. emotionally poignant, then we could. change the narrative around what people. thought when they looked at an Asian. family. We could be ambassadors. And. maybe Asians could be seen as what. studio execs like to call wish. fulfillment or. aspirational. And it worked. We set the. temperature and the weather changed. and. I was proud to call myself an. Asian-American. filmmaker. It became not only the first. all Asian Hollywood studio movie cast in. 25 years, but was the highest grossing. romantic comedy in a decade. It became a. box office juggernaut. Changed the. landscape of what studios was willing to. do. And since then, incredible Asian. filmmakers, writers, actors, producers. have been flourishing, making studio. lots a lot of money. Now, when I started. in this business, I made a painting. It. was called Resilience. It was a It was. in my living room. It was a tree with. empty branches. And every time something. good happened, I would paint a little. leaf on it. And this continued until. well, I moved apartments. It was too big. to move. And uh it wasn't very pretty,. so I left it. But before I threw it out,. I did paint the tree just to feel. complete. It will take many leaves for. you to feel. full-ish. But it will be worth the. journey. Trees lose their leaves every. year, yet they stand tall and wait for. better days. I saw that on a meme on. someone's Insta.

story. I thought, "This is so dumb, but. so true." So, I gave it to you guys. So,. this year with Wicked, I got my first. real real invitation to the Oscars. No. kinkos. And as the movie was nominated. for 10 awards that night, including best. picture, I wore Emerald Green, sitting. fourth row next to Michelle Yo and my. wife. And as we sat there waiting for. the show to begin, I remember feeling so. calm, more calm than at any industry. party where I feel like I need to prove. my membership. Here I was surrendered,. full breaths, fully. present. And as I looked around, I felt. like the whole room darkened like it did. when I would look through that. viewfinder. My wife nervously tapping. her heel on the seat. Jeff Goldlum's. wide eyes as he's humming to himself. behind me. He does that. Demi Moore. gracefully sliding to her seat. It felt. like silence. And Michelle grasped my. hand. Just one year earlier, she had won. her best actress Oscar, breaking the. glass ceiling for Asian women in. Hollywood. Yeah. And she grasped my. hand. She's the one who grasped my hand. And then the drapes ascended and a. rainbow appeared. And Ariana Grande in. ruby red. Our Galinda the good glowing. effervescently through the scrim. And. then the music began. And I knew I was. home. Not at the Oscars, but deep within. myself. And she sang, "Somewhere over. the rainbow, way up high. There's a land. that I heard of once in a lullabi. Somewhere over the rainbow, skies are. blue. And the dreams that you dare to. dream really do come true." And I click. my heels three times, and I'm back down. to earth right here with you at the. coliseum on your big day in the shadow. of greatness. and with my daughter. existing in a world where Cynthia Revo's. alphabet defies. gravity and I get the honor of speaking. to 50,000 of you dreamers as a filmmaker. and a. father a far cry from that silly. immature boy who avoided the coliseum. all those years. ago. I know we are supposed to say. conquer, win, be the. best, but I've seen the other side of. the rainbow. And nothing is better than. knowing you've lived a great story. I. hope you get to. And as I wrote this. speech, I had a hard time figuring out. what to say to close things out. But as. a parent, I realized all I wanted to say. to you was what I would say to my. daughter as she goes off into the world. And that is. this. You have an extraordinary story,. every single one of you. And I deeply,. deeply believe in you. You will go. through tough times, terrifying times. even, but your beautiful brain will. figure out creative ways to get out of. it. And your ferocity will get you. through it. You will feel deep. love and deep. heartache. And you will have profound. questions, many that never get answered,. the greatest gift that God gives you. And I hope that you embrace every single. one of them, those moments, because they. are all reminders that you are. alive. And just so you. know, that American. dream, I think it does exist. It. does. And it's just not just an American. dream. I think it's a world. dream. It's just that there is more than. one road to get there. And many are. being paved right now as we speak. And I. will do everything in my power as I'm.

assume everyone here and everyone in the. stands too to ensure that they remain. there when you arrive so that you too. can feel like you're home. Fight on. Class of. 2025. Truly fight on.

item.description:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 了解一位知名導演如何將個人成長故事，從初戀失利到找到人生熱情，轉化為對應屆畢業生的深刻啟示。
  • 洞悉在充滿不確定性（如疫情、經濟危機、環境災難）的時代，新世代所面臨的獨特挑戰與改造世界的機會。
  • 學習「說故事的力量」如何超越知識傳遞，能引發同理心、促成行動，並幫助人們重新定義自我與周遭世界。

摘要

演講者Chu博士回到母校體育場，這個地方對他意義深重，既有美好的回憶，也曾是他情感受挫的見證。他憶起當年高中女友在他入學一年後也考進同一所學校，原本期待能一起「稱霸」校園，沒想到第一天就被甩，女友還和明星美式足球員交往。這讓他一度難以踏足校園甚至不敢來看比賽。然而，當那位明星球員在一次關鍵比賽中失手錯失致勝球時，身為校友的他雖感遺憾，但18歲不成熟的他卻為此度過了「人生中最美好的一週」。這個有點複雜的開場，讓聽眾感受到他的真誠與幽默。

Chu博士接著分享他如今的家庭，他擁有一位傑出的太太克莉絲汀和五個年幼的孩子，笑稱太太「幸好」對運動員沒興趣，反而喜歡他這種有點中年發福的亞裔男性，幽默地將自己的「老爹身材」歸功於大學時期對碳水化合物的熱愛。他對能帶家人回到南加州大學（USC）感到興奮，因為校園的每一處都承載著他成長的記憶，那些塑造了今天他的點點滴滴。從宿舍生活到發現自己對酒精過敏，再到對碳水化合物的熱愛，甚至是偷偷溜進奧斯卡頒獎典禮，這些經歷都構築了他的一部分。他也特別提到他學生時期短片攝影師，在二十二年後成為他最新電影《威卡》（Wicked）的攝影指導，強調了長久的連結。

他對眼前充滿活力的畢業生們表達了祝賀，提醒他們此刻不僅是他們自己成功，也是他們身邊那些了解他們所有缺點、所有過錯，卻依然愛著他們的人們的成功。Chu博士承認，在準備這次演講時，他深入思考了這一代畢業生所經歷的一切：新冠疫情、金融危機、環境災難等等。儘管如此，他們依然堅持下來，完成了學業，這讓他和所有人都為之驕傲。

然而，他認為這些畢業生即將面對更為艱鉅的任務。身為一個說故事的人，他起初不確定自己能給予什麼建議，甚至開玩笑說學校應該找一位世界領袖或發明家來演講。但越是思考，他越是覺得自己人生中的某些經驗或許能有所幫助，因為「我們的世界正在碰撞」。無論未來從事何種行業，未來都是相互連結的，並且需要「真實地面對」，以便做好準備。他相信這一代人面臨著前所未有的深刻任務，不僅僅是繼承一個變動的世界，更要從根本上定義我們將成為誰，去重新想像世界，並為未來設定基礎。他形容我們正處於一個「舊故事正在崩塌」的時刻，恐懼、指責和分裂似乎佔據主導，但這同時也是一個深刻的「機會」，因為當舊故事瓦解，正是時候書寫一個新故事。

Chu博士分享了他的「故事」的開端：加州洛斯阿圖斯一家名為「主廚周」的中國餐廳。他的父母是來自台灣的移民，於1969年創業並經營至今，以舒適的價格提供家常菜。他形容父母是「美國夢」的活生生體現，儘管剛來美國時英語有限，但他們熱愛美國文化，並渴望孩子們能融入社會。他的父母將「奧茲巫師」視為不朽的美國童話，教導他們只要遵循黃磚路—信念、紀律、勤奮工作並熱愛所做之事—就能實現夢想。在餐廳裡，父母教導他們要成為文化的「大使」，不僅要滿足顧客的胃，更要贏得尊重，改變人們對華裔家庭的看法。

每年奧斯卡頒獎典禮是家中的盛事，他們會圍坐電視前觀看，對那些好萊塢名人充滿敬畏與嚮往。然而，從餐廳的餐桌到好萊塢，似乎是一條遙不可及的路，一個沒有任何背景、看起來不像他們家人能進入的世界。直到有一天，他找到了一個「鏡頭」：一台小型的Sony攝影機。當他透過觀景窗看世界時，周遭的一切都黯淡下來，只剩下一個小小的光框。他那過度活躍的思緒平靜下來，時間彷彿變慢，他「屈服於當下」，並透過這個框架發現了那些日常生活中原本看不到的「瞬間」和「細節」。

這個發現就像他的「秘密」，但當他在電視上播放錄像時，這個秘密彷彿可以與家人共享。即使家人會笑著抱怨鏡頭晃動，但他們是「與他同在」的。他發現這台機器正在「召喚同理心」。他用攝影機編輯了一個家庭旅遊影片，加上音樂後在客廳播放，家人們看著螢幕上的自己，眼睛被緊緊吸引。幾分鐘後，他們流下了眼淚。他們在螢幕上看到了那個「實現美國夢」的家庭，他們的夢想以畫面形式傳承給下一代。透過剪輯，他讓他們對未來的一切希望變得「真實可信」。

這個經歷讓他著迷。更重要的是，他們不僅僅是看到了螢幕上的家庭，他們「也變成了」螢幕上的那個家庭。儘管旅行的細節他們早已知曉，但真正讓他們至今仍津津樂道的是觀看影片時的「感受」。螢幕上的一個畫面，賦予了他們的家庭「價值」，為他們往後的日子設定了敘事。這是一個「啟示」：一個隨機的片段可以組織成一個故事，引發情感反應，產生同理心，進而促使觀看者採取行動。在今天這個「知識唾手可得」的世界裡，這點尤其重要，也是他今晚極力想傳達給畢業生的訊息。

================================================

Now, it's my honor to invite Dr. Chu to. deliver the commencement address for the. class of. 2025. Dr. Chu, I like. it. How many Dr. Chs are there in this. stadium right now? There's probably a. lot. Good evening, everybody. It feels. good to be back at the Coliseum. Yes. 50,000 plus Trojans in prime time. This is my. home. I'd walk here for games every. Saturday, kicking that flag pole on the. way. But I also have a complicated. relationship with this place. Uh, so you. see, my high school girlfriend at the. time got into SC a year after I did. And. uh, I was I was so psyched. I mean, this. was going to be our school to. rule. And then on the first day, she. dumped. me. I felt the same. I felt Thank you. I. I I I I I hear you. And then she started. dating the star football player here. H I know it's conflicting cuz it's like,. oh, he's so handsome, but also. ah suddenly I couldn't walk to class. without the fear I'd run into them. together so cute on their bikes. And I. so I had to take alternative routes to. class and I stopped coming to. games. And then one Saturday, USC was. playing a big rival school which I will. not name. It was the final seconds down to a few. points. They throw the ball to this guy. for the game-winning touchdown and he. misses the. ball. No, no, no, no, no. The whole. campus was devastated. And as I, as a. Trojan, was. too, but as an 18-year-old immature boy,. I had the best week of my life. So, I. mean it when I say it's good to be back. in the. coliseum. Yeah. Now, now I have the most. extraordinary wife, Kristen, who's over. there tonight. And. lucky, she almost nicks that joke for. me, but it's okay. And she said, lucky. for me, she is not into. athletes. She's more into middle-aged. Asian guys with dad bods. So, and now she's married to a doctor. So, you're welcome, babe. We we we've got five kids. Uh a. seven-year-old, a 5-year-old, a. three-year-old, a 2-year-old, and a. six-month-old. So, she deserves all the applause. Yes. And and I am excited to have my. family here at USC because this place. means so much to me. Every inch of the. campus holds a memory or three chapters. of of my time here that built the pieces. of who I am today. Like my freshman. year, I lived over at Mark's Tower. Yeah. What about those Marks Tower. people? That's great. I'd spend Thursday. nights at Tratties where I learned I was. allergic to. alcohol. Tuesday nights at the Pasta. Roma where I learned I love. carbs. It's the beginning of my dad. bought. And I even snuck into the Oscars. when they had them at the shrine. I made. a fake pass. Kinkos laminated. They. can't take this away from me, right? Okay. The cinematographer of my student film,. Alice Brooks, just shot my latest film,. Wicked. 22 plus years. take it in tonight. Look at how. beautiful you are. You made it. Look at. all your loved ones around you or. texting you. They made it,. too. And they know all your flaws. call me John John. And they loved they. loved movies like movies on all the time. at the house. For context, this was a. Reagan's '8s America where Steven. Crazy Rich Asians, we resisted going. with a streaming company because we felt. like putting this movie on the big. screen would tell a story in itself. And we felt like it's not just the. movie, but the campaign itself could. tell that story. And if the movie was. actually funny and romantic and. emotionally poignant, then we could. change the narrative around what people. thought when they looked at an Asian. family. We could be ambassadors. And. maybe Asians could be seen as what. studio execs like to call wish. fulfillment or. aspirational. And it worked. We set the. temperature and the weather changed. and. I was proud to call myself an. Asian-American. filmmaker. It became not only the first. all Asian Hollywood studio movie cast in. 25 years, but was the highest grossing. romantic comedy in a decade. It became a. box office juggernaut. Changed the. landscape of what studios was willing to. do. And since then, incredible Asian. filmmakers, writers, actors, producers. have been flourishing, making studio. lots a lot of money. Now, when I started. in this business, I made a painting. It. was called Resilience. It was a It was. in my living room. It was a tree with. empty branches. And every time something. good happened, I would paint a little. leaf on it. And this continued until. well, I moved apartments. It was too big. to move. And uh it wasn't very pretty,. so I left it. But before I threw it out,. I did paint the tree just to feel. complete. It will take many leaves for. you to feel. full-ish. But it will be worth the. journey. Trees lose their leaves every. year, yet they stand tall and wait for. better days. I saw that on a meme on. someone's Insta.

story. I thought, "This is so dumb, but. so true." So, I gave it to you guys. So,. this year with Wicked, I got my first. real real invitation to the Oscars. No. kinkos. And as the movie was nominated. for 10 awards that night, including best. picture, I wore Emerald Green, sitting. fourth row next to Michelle Yo and my. wife. And as we sat there waiting for. the show to begin, I remember feeling so. calm, more calm than at any industry. party where I feel like I need to prove. my membership. Here I was surrendered,. full breaths, fully. present. And as I looked around, I felt. like the whole room darkened like it did. when I would look through that. viewfinder. My wife nervously tapping. her heel on the seat. Jeff Goldlum's. wide eyes as he's humming to himself. behind me. He does that. Demi Moore. gracefully sliding to her seat. It felt. like silence. And Michelle grasped my. hand. Just one year earlier, she had won. her best actress Oscar, breaking the. glass ceiling for Asian women in. Hollywood. Yeah. And she grasped my. hand. She's the one who grasped my hand. And then the drapes ascended and a. rainbow appeared. And Ariana Grande in. ruby red. Our Galinda the good glowing. effervescently through the scrim. And. then the music began. And I knew I was. home. Not at the Oscars, but deep within. myself. And she sang, "Somewhere over. the rainbow, way up high. There's a land. that I heard of once in a lullabi. Somewhere over the rainbow, skies are. blue. And the dreams that you dare to. dream really do come true." And I click. my heels three times, and I'm back down. to earth right here with you at the. coliseum on your big day in the shadow. of greatness. and with my daughter. existing in a world where Cynthia Revo's. alphabet defies. gravity and I get the honor of speaking. to 50,000 of you dreamers as a filmmaker. and a. father a far cry from that silly. immature boy who avoided the coliseum. all those years. ago. I know we are supposed to say. conquer, win, be the. best, but I've seen the other side of. the rainbow. And nothing is better than. knowing you've lived a great story. I. hope you get to. And as I wrote this. speech, I had a hard time figuring out. what to say to close things out. But as. a parent, I realized all I wanted to say. to you was what I would say to my. daughter as she goes off into the world. And that is. this. You have an extraordinary story,. every single one of you. And I deeply,. deeply believe in you. You will go. through tough times, terrifying times. even, but your beautiful brain will. figure out creative ways to get out of. it. And your ferocity will get you. through it. You will feel deep. love and deep. heartache. And you will have profound. questions, many that never get answered,. the greatest gift that God gives you. And I hope that you embrace every single. one of them, those moments, because they. are all reminders that you are. alive. And just so you. know, that American. dream, I think it does exist. It. does. And it's just not just an American. dream. I think it's a world. dream. It's just that there is more than. one road to get there. And many are. being paved right now as we speak. And I. will do everything in my power as I'm.

assume everyone here and everyone in the. stands too to ensure that they remain. there when you arrive so that you too. can feel like you're home. Fight on. Class of. 2025. Truly fight on.

item.content:encoded:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 了解一位知名導演如何將個人成長故事，從初戀失利到找到人生熱情，轉化為對應屆畢業生的深刻啟示。
  • 洞悉在充滿不確定性（如疫情、經濟危機、環境災難）的時代，新世代所面臨的獨特挑戰與改造世界的機會。
  • 學習「說故事的力量」如何超越知識傳遞，能引發同理心、促成行動，並幫助人們重新定義自我與周遭世界。

摘要

演講者Chu博士回到母校體育場，這個地方對他意義深重，既有美好的回憶，也曾是他情感受挫的見證。他憶起當年高中女友在他入學一年後也考進同一所學校，原本期待能一起「稱霸」校園，沒想到第一天就被甩，女友還和明星美式足球員交往。這讓他一度難以踏足校園甚至不敢來看比賽。然而，當那位明星球員在一次關鍵比賽中失手錯失致勝球時，身為校友的他雖感遺憾，但18歲不成熟的他卻為此度過了「人生中最美好的一週」。這個有點複雜的開場，讓聽眾感受到他的真誠與幽默。

Chu博士接著分享他如今的家庭，他擁有一位傑出的太太克莉絲汀和五個年幼的孩子，笑稱太太「幸好」對運動員沒興趣，反而喜歡他這種有點中年發福的亞裔男性，幽默地將自己的「老爹身材」歸功於大學時期對碳水化合物的熱愛。他對能帶家人回到南加州大學（USC）感到興奮，因為校園的每一處都承載著他成長的記憶，那些塑造了今天他的點點滴滴。從宿舍生活到發現自己對酒精過敏，再到對碳水化合物的熱愛，甚至是偷偷溜進奧斯卡頒獎典禮，這些經歷都構築了他的一部分。他也特別提到他學生時期短片攝影師，在二十二年後成為他最新電影《威卡》（Wicked）的攝影指導，強調了長久的連結。

他對眼前充滿活力的畢業生們表達了祝賀，提醒他們此刻不僅是他們自己成功，也是他們身邊那些了解他們所有缺點、所有過錯，卻依然愛著他們的人們的成功。Chu博士承認，在準備這次演講時，他深入思考了這一代畢業生所經歷的一切：新冠疫情、金融危機、環境災難等等。儘管如此，他們依然堅持下來，完成了學業，這讓他和所有人都為之驕傲。

然而，他認為這些畢業生即將面對更為艱鉅的任務。身為一個說故事的人，他起初不確定自己能給予什麼建議，甚至開玩笑說學校應該找一位世界領袖或發明家來演講。但越是思考，他越是覺得自己人生中的某些經驗或許能有所幫助，因為「我們的世界正在碰撞」。無論未來從事何種行業，未來都是相互連結的，並且需要「真實地面對」，以便做好準備。他相信這一代人面臨著前所未有的深刻任務，不僅僅是繼承一個變動的世界，更要從根本上定義我們將成為誰，去重新想像世界，並為未來設定基礎。他形容我們正處於一個「舊故事正在崩塌」的時刻，恐懼、指責和分裂似乎佔據主導，但這同時也是一個深刻的「機會」，因為當舊故事瓦解，正是時候書寫一個新故事。

Chu博士分享了他的「故事」的開端：加州洛斯阿圖斯一家名為「主廚周」的中國餐廳。他的父母是來自台灣的移民，於1969年創業並經營至今，以舒適的價格提供家常菜。他形容父母是「美國夢」的活生生體現，儘管剛來美國時英語有限，但他們熱愛美國文化，並渴望孩子們能融入社會。他的父母將「奧茲巫師」視為不朽的美國童話，教導他們只要遵循黃磚路—信念、紀律、勤奮工作並熱愛所做之事—就能實現夢想。在餐廳裡，父母教導他們要成為文化的「大使」，不僅要滿足顧客的胃，更要贏得尊重，改變人們對華裔家庭的看法。

每年奧斯卡頒獎典禮是家中的盛事，他們會圍坐電視前觀看，對那些好萊塢名人充滿敬畏與嚮往。然而，從餐廳的餐桌到好萊塢，似乎是一條遙不可及的路，一個沒有任何背景、看起來不像他們家人能進入的世界。直到有一天，他找到了一個「鏡頭」：一台小型的Sony攝影機。當他透過觀景窗看世界時，周遭的一切都黯淡下來，只剩下一個小小的光框。他那過度活躍的思緒平靜下來，時間彷彿變慢，他「屈服於當下」，並透過這個框架發現了那些日常生活中原本看不到的「瞬間」和「細節」。

這個發現就像他的「秘密」，但當他在電視上播放錄像時，這個秘密彷彿可以與家人共享。即使家人會笑著抱怨鏡頭晃動，但他們是「與他同在」的。他發現這台機器正在「召喚同理心」。他用攝影機編輯了一個家庭旅遊影片，加上音樂後在客廳播放，家人們看著螢幕上的自己，眼睛被緊緊吸引。幾分鐘後，他們流下了眼淚。他們在螢幕上看到了那個「實現美國夢」的家庭，他們的夢想以畫面形式傳承給下一代。透過剪輯，他讓他們對未來的一切希望變得「真實可信」。

這個經歷讓他著迷。更重要的是，他們不僅僅是看到了螢幕上的家庭，他們「也變成了」螢幕上的那個家庭。儘管旅行的細節他們早已知曉，但真正讓他們至今仍津津樂道的是觀看影片時的「感受」。螢幕上的一個畫面，賦予了他們的家庭「價值」，為他們往後的日子設定了敘事。這是一個「啟示」：一個隨機的片段可以組織成一個故事，引發情感反應，產生同理心，進而促使觀看者採取行動。在今天這個「知識唾手可得」的世界裡，這點尤其重要，也是他今晚極力想傳達給畢業生的訊息。

================================================

item.title: 世界首富大戰美國總統，馬斯克川普鬧翻的真相！到底發生了什麼...
item.itunes:title: 世界首富大戰美國總統，馬斯克川普鬧翻的真相！到底發生了什麼...
item.itunes:author: 公子沈 Mr. Shen
item.itunes:summary:
值得閱讀的理由

  • 這段影片深入分析了世界首富馬斯克與美國前總統川普之間公開決裂的具體原因和背後的深層政治考量，而非僅停留在表面衝突。
  • 內容揭示了美國政治運作的複雜性，說明了總統決策需平衡多方利益，以及國會政治對政策制定的影響，反駁了總統是獨裁者的簡單觀點。
  • 分析了馬斯克作為一位具有理想主義色彩的商人，如何權衡個人理念、商業利益與政治現實，並推測他公開與川普切割是為了其長遠的政治和商業佈局。
  • 探討了雙方衝突的導火線，包括川普簽署的預算案在減稅、增加國防開支及國債問題上的分歧，以及電動車補貼和NASA人選等具體爭議。

摘要

衝突的導火線：預算案的分歧

在最新的公子時評中，主持人公子沈探討了世界首富馬斯克與美國前總統川普之間近期公開的嚴重衝突。這場爭執的導火線源於川普正式簽署的一份政府預算案，該法案已在眾議院通過。這份預算案的關鍵內容包括大幅度減稅、增加國防開支，以及導致美國國債持續攀升。

馬斯克為何反對？理想與現實的碰撞

公子沈指出，馬斯克強烈反對這份預算案，其核心理由在於他認為這將使美國國債持續增加。馬斯克過去曾深入美國政府部門，試圖透過大規模裁員和削減政府開支來減少國債，他認為國債會導致美國政府破產，不應留給下一代。川普簽署的預算案不僅未能減少國債，反而增加，同時取消了對電動車的補貼（這直接影響到馬斯克的事業，如特斯拉），卻保留了對傳統能源的補貼。這讓馬斯克感到過去的努力白費，並且認為這是不公平的，是對他的背叛。

川普的視角：政治現實與利益平衡

從川普的角度來看，他認為自己過去給予了馬斯克許多支持和好處，而馬斯克現在卻反對他最重要的預算案，這是不可接受的。川普認為馬斯克此舉等同於與民主黨站在一起批評他。公子沈解釋，雖然川普也希望減少債務，但美國的政治體系非常複雜。總統並非獨裁者，決策需要平衡各方利益，特別是來自共和黨內部、國會（參議院和眾議院）以及不同利益集團的壓力。減稅是共和黨的重要理念，而大幅削減政府開支（特別是公務員體系）在政治上非常困難。這份預算案已是川普和共和黨在多方妥協下所能通過的「最好方案」，任何大幅修改都可能導致法案流產。因此，為了推動核心政策（如減稅），川普不得不犧牲部分目標（如嚴控國債）。

個人恩怨與政治策略：NASA人選及人身攻擊

除了預算案，導致衝突惡化的還有其他因素。例如，在NASA負責人的人選問題上，馬斯克支持一位他認為有經驗的人士，而川普偏好自己的人選，這也讓馬斯克不滿。更嚴重的是，雙方的衝突甚至升級到人身攻擊。馬斯克公開暗示川普與愛潑斯坦（Epstein）的性販賣網絡有關，這觸碰了川普最敏感的神經，因為這不僅攻擊了他的名聲，也可能激怒整個共和黨，是極具破壞性的指控。

馬斯克的真正目的：長遠佈局與切割

公子沈分析，馬斯克是一位精明的商人，他的行為不是一時衝動，而是經過計算的。他公開與川普和共和黨決裂，甚至揚言成立第三政黨，其背後可能有更深層的策略。他認為，馬斯克的目的是為了與川普切割，為自己爭取未來的政治空間。馬斯克意識到川普的政治壽命可能有限（任期限制，以及未來可能的法律和彈劾問題），而他本人還有數十年的影響力。透過公開衝突，他向外界（尤其是民主黨）表明他並非完全與共和黨綁定，增加了未來與其他黨派合作的可能性。這種策略雖然可能導致短期損失（如特斯拉股價下跌），但從長遠來看，可以保護他的企業免受未來執政黨的打壓，並提升他作為獨立第三方的影響力。

結論：複雜的博弈與不同的目標

公子沈總結道，馬斯克與川普之間的衝突是各自追求不同目標和在複雜政治環境下進行博弈的結果。馬斯克著眼於其理想主義和長遠的商業及政治影響力（他的政治遺產），而川普作為總統，必須在黨派利益和政策目標之間尋求平衡。理解這場衝突，需要超越簡單的情緒化指責，而是分析雙方各自的訴求、所處的政治位置以及他們試圖達成的目標。

=============

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_GtqjfPlwp0

世界首富大戰美國總統，#川普 #馬斯克 終於翻臉了！到底發生了什麼？【#公子時評 #公子沈 第2692期】

  • 內容揭示了美國政治運作的複雜性，說明了總統決策需平衡多方利益，以及國會政治對政策制定的影響，反駁了總統是獨裁者的簡單觀點。
  • 分析了馬斯克作為一位具有理想主義色彩的商人，如何權衡個人理念、商業利益與政治現實，並推測他公開與川普切割是為了其長遠的政治和商業佈局。
  • 探討了雙方衝突的導火線，包括川普簽署的預算案在減稅、增加國防開支及國債問題上的分歧，以及電動車補貼和NASA人選等具體爭議。
